COVERS: §3 (RaptorQ Foundation) + §3.4 (Integration Points) + §3.5 (ECS Substrate)

### Scenario 1: GF(256) Arithmetic

### Scenario 2: RaptorQ Encode/Decode
- test_e2e_raptorq_encode_decode_small: Encode 10 source symbols, lose 2, decode from any 10 of remaining, verify
- test_e2e_raptorq_encode_decode_large: Encode 10000 symbols (large database), verify decode with overhead < 2%

### Scenario 4: ECS Object Lifecycle
- test_e2e_ecs_object_create_retrieve: Create ECS object (commit capsule), store as symbols, retrieve and decode

- test_e2e_ecs_root_manifest_bootstrap: Write RootManifest, shutdown, reopen, verify bootstrap recovery

### Scenario 5: Replication (Fountain-Coded)
- test_e2e_replication_sender_receiver: One node sends symbols, another receives and reconstructs database

 {"id":"bd-m1nte","title":"I2: Adaptive flush interval — tune flush frequency based on workload pattern","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:26:46.681980906Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T17:14:54.771828142Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T17:14:54.771514014Z","close_reason":"Implemented adaptive flush interval: 256 for pure writes, 16 for mixed, immediate on error","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-m1nte","depends_on_id":"bd-otbu1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:26:47.218052985Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3375,"issue_id":"bd-m1nte","author":"ubuntu","text":"## What
Instead of fixed 64-statement flush interval, adaptively tune:
- Pure INSERT workload: flush every 256 statements (high throughput)
- Mixed read/write: flush every 16 statements (read-after-write consistency)
- Error recovery: flush immediately after any error

## Where
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs: adaptive_flush_interval() method
- Track statement types in retained txn: n_writes, n_reads, last_error

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Pure INSERT autocommit ≤ 3x slower than C SQLite
2. Mixed workload correctness (read-after-write always consistent)
3. No visible behavior change to user

 {"id":"bd-m35ht","title":"W10: Same-connection read-your-own-write overlay — eliminate retained autocommit flush on reads","description":"## What
In mixed OLTP (80% read / 20% write), each SELECT after an autocommit INSERT triggers flush_retained_autocommit_txn_for_read() + MemDB reload. This costs ~500-1000ns per read and is the primary cause of the 22x mixed OLTP gap.

## Why This Is Critical
Mixed OLTP is the most realistic workload. Pure INSERT benchmarks are synthetic. Real apps do INSERT then immediately SELECT. The read-triggered flush destroys all autocommit batching gains.

## How (per Codex suggestion)
Create a per-connection shadow publication so same-connection reads see retained writes without global commit:
1. Reads from the SAME connection that wrote can resolve against the retained write-set directly
2. Only flush when: (a) another connection reads, (b) explicit COMMIT, (c) connection close
3. The write-set is the private snapshot — reads check write-set first, then committed snapshot

This is essentially read-your-own-writes (RYOW) consistency within a single connection.

## Implementation
- In the read path (execute_statement for SELECT), check if the connection has a retained autocommit txn with dirty tables
- If the SELECT touches dirty tables: instead of flushing, resolve page reads against the retained write_set first
- This is already how explicit transactions work (VDBE reads from active_txn write_set)
- For autocommit: extend this to the retained_autocommit_txn

## Files
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs — flush_retained_autocommit_txn_for_read, execute_statement read path

## Estimated impact
22x mixed OLTP → ~8-10x. This is the highest-EV fix for real-world workloads.

 {"id":"bd-m5rpg","title":"Implement VALUES clause in VDBE codegen","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-27T11:19:35.388530805Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-27T11:21:27.390692829Z","closed_at":"2026-02-27T11:21:27.390673603Z","close_reason":"VALUES clause in VDBE codegen — 4 new tests, 1218 total passing","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":1822,"issue_id":"bd-m5rpg","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Implement VALUES Clause in VDBE Codegen

### Problem
Standalone `VALUES` clauses (e.g. `VALUES (1, 'a'), (2, 'b')`) failed in
the VDBE codegen path with `CodegenError::Unsupported("VALUES in SELECT")`.

### Fix
Added `codegen_values_select()` function that generates a minimal VDBE
program: `Init -> Transaction -> [for each row: eval exprs -> ResultRow] -> Halt`.

- Each row's expressions are evaluated via `emit_expr` with `ctx=None`
- Multiple rows emit multiple `ResultRow` instructions
- Column count determined from first row
- Handles literals, expressions, NULL, and function calls in VALUES

### Files Changed
- `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/codegen.rs` — Added `codegen_values_select`,
  routed `SelectCore::Values` to it
- `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` — 4 new tests

### Tests (4 new, all pass)
- `test_values_clause_single_row` — VALUES (1, 'hello', 3.14)
- `test_values_clause_multiple_rows` — VALUES (1,'a'), (2,'b'), (3,'c')
- `test_values_clause_with_expressions` — VALUES (1+2, abs(-5))
- `test_values_clause_with_null` — VALUES (NULL, 42)

### Quality Gates
- 1218 fsqlite-core tests pass (0 regressions, +4 new)

 {"id":"bd-mb7gl","title":"P1: Prefetch child pages during B-tree descent — hide L3/DRAM latency","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:17:26.316143031Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T03:27:17.630342591Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T03:27:17.630110827Z","close_reason":"Committed in 9abedc20: prefetch_l1_read() in cursor.rs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":3391,"issue_id":"bd-mb7gl","author":"ubuntu","text":"## What
In cursor.rs, during interior node traversal, after determining which child to descend to, issue a software prefetch for that child's page data BEFORE doing the binary search on the current node.

## Where
- crates/fsqlite-btree/src/cursor.rs: in the descent loop, after loading an interior page
- Add: std::arch::x86_64::_mm_prefetch(child_page_ptr, _MM_HINT_T0)
- For :memory: mode: child_page_ptr = &fast_pages[child_pgno]

## Why This Works
Binary search on a 4KB page takes ~50-100 ns (5-6 comparisons × 10-15 ns each). 
L3 cache load takes ~50 ns. If we prefetch BEFORE binary search, the child page 
arrives by the time we need it. Net effect: memory latency is hidden behind computation.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. B-tree descent microbenchmark: ≥ 10% faster for random-key lookups
2. No regression for sequential inserts (already fast via append path)
3. No incorrect prefetches (verify page number is valid before prefetching)
","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:17:26Z"},{"id":3418,"issue_id":"bd-mb7gl","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Risk & Fallback
**Risk**: Prefetching invalid addresses could cause SEGFAULT on some platforms.
**Mitigation**: ALWAYS validate page number before prefetching: `if pgno < max_page_count { prefetch(&pages[pgno]) }`. Software prefetch of an invalid address is a no-op on x86_64 (silently ignored), but better to be safe.
**Fallback**: If prefetching shows no measurable benefit (< 5% improvement on cold-cache workload), remove it — the overhead is near-zero but the code complexity isn't worth it for no gain.

Acceptance criteria: all 23 crates described with enough specificity for onboarding; descriptions do not contradict dependency layers in §8.1-§8.2.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T05:02:38.969878831Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T15:51:57.554521957Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T15:51:57.554471814Z","close_reason":"Completed §8.3 compliance implementation: unit/e2e identifiers wired plus deterministic e2e doc-compliance runner with structured logging","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-sxm2","depends_on_id":"bd-1wwc","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-sxm2","depends_on_id":"bd-3an","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1998,"issue_id":"bd-sxm2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## §8.3 Per-Crate Detailed Descriptions

### fsqlite-types (~3,500 LOC)
Foundational types, zero internal deps. Modules: page.rs (PageNumber/NonZeroU32, PageBuf/PageData page-aligned, PageSize), value.rs (SqliteValue enum), opcode.rs (190+ Opcodes + OpcodeInfo), serial.rs (SerialType encoding/decoding), record.rs (Record struct, header, ser/de), txn.rs (TxnId u64, TxnMode enum), flags.rs (OpenFlags, SyncFlags, AccessFlags, LockLevel), limits.rs (SQLITE_MAX_*), affinity.rs (TypeAffinity), collation.rs (CollationId, BINARY/NOCASE/RTRIM). ~80 types, all Debug+Clone, most Copy.

### fsqlite-error (~800 LOC)
Error types via thiserror. FrankenError enum (~40 variants), ErrorCode (~30 primary), extended codes (SQLITE_BUSY_RECOVERY etc.), Result type alias. Every variant carries context (operation, page/table, source error).

### fsqlite-vfs (~2,500 LOC)
VFS abstraction = sqlite3_vfs + sqlite3_io_methods. traits.rs (Vfs/VfsFile), memory.rs (MemoryVfs with HashMap<PathBuf, Arc<Mutex<Vec<u8>>>>), unix.rs (UnixVfs via asupersync blocking I/O, fcntl F_SETLK, 5 lock levels), flags.rs (VfsOpenFlags). Deps: fsqlite-types, fsqlite-error, asupersync.

### fsqlite-pager (~4,000 LOC)
Page cache + txn state machine. pager.rs (state: Open->Reader->Writer->Error, manages db file + journal + ARC cache, defines MvccPager + CheckpointPageWriter traits), cache.rs (ArcCache impl S6), page_ref.rs (RAII PageRef, decrements ref_count on drop), journal.rs (rollback journal, hot journal detection), state.rs (PagerState enum), header.rs (100-byte db header). Deps: fsqlite-vfs, fsqlite-types, fsqlite-error.

### fsqlite-wal (~3,500 LOC)
WAL implementation. wal.rs (Wal struct, header parsing, frame append, cumulative checksum S7.1), frame.rs (WalFrame 24B header + page data), index.rs (WalIndex SHM hash table, linear probing, reader marks, lock bytes), checkpoint.rs (PASSIVE/FULL/RESTART/TRUNCATE), recovery.rs (checksum chain validation, committed txn replay, RaptorQ self-healing), raptorq.rs (repair symbol gen on commit, decode during recovery). Deps: fsqlite-vfs, asupersync. Does NOT depend on fsqlite-pager (cycle broken via CheckpointPageWriter trait).

### fsqlite-mvcc (~3,000 LOC)
MVCC version management — heart of concurrency innovation. manager.rs (MvccManager: txns, version store, lock table, commit index, witness hooks, GC), snapshot.rs (Snapshot{high, schema_epoch}, capture, visibility predicate), version.rs (PageVersion, arena-backed version chains), lock_table.rs (InProcessPageLockTable sharded HashMap + ShmPageLockTable adapter), transaction.rs (Transaction lifecycle Active->Committed/Aborted, write set, intent log, witness keys), commit.rs (FCW via CommitIndex + merge ladder, coordinator publication), gc.rs (horizon, chain pruning, reclaimability), coordinator.rs (WriteCoordinator wrapping asupersync two-phase MPSC). Deps: fsqlite-wal, fsqlite-pager, parking_lot, asupersync.

### fsqlite-btree (~5,000 LOC)
B-tree storage engine, most complex after VDBE. cursor.rs (BtCursor, page stack max depth 20@4KB/40@512B, save/restore), cell.rs (IntKeyCell, BlobKeyCell, InteriorCell, varint), balance.rs (balance_nonroot redistribution, balance_deeper new root, balance_quick fast append), overflow.rs (overflow page chains), free_list.rs (trunk/leaf, allocate/grow/deallocate), payload.rs (BtreePayload spanning local+overflow), table.rs (table B-tree intkey ops), index.rs (index B-tree blobkey ops). Deps: fsqlite-pager (MvccPager trait), fsqlite-types.

### fsqlite-ast (~2,000 LOC)
SQL AST nodes. stmt.rs (~20 Statement variants), expr.rs (~30 Expr variants), select.rs (SelectStatement, SelectCore enum with Select{}/Values(), CompoundOp, JoinClause, OrderingTerm, LimitClause, WithClause, Cte), table_ref.rs (TableRef enum), ddl.rs (ColumnDef, TableConstraint, IndexedColumn, ForeignKeyClause), literal.rs (Literal enum incl CurrentTime/Date/Timestamp), operator.rs (BinaryOp, UnaryOp), span.rs (Span byte offset range). All nodes carry Span.

### fsqlite-parser (~4,500 LOC)
Lexer + recursive descent. lexer.rs (~150 TokenType variants, memchr-accelerated, line/column tracking), parser.rs (one method per production, Pratt precedence for expressions), keyword.rs (perfect hash for 150+ keywords via phf), error.rs (parse errors with span, expected tokens, recovery hints).

### fsqlite-planner (~3,000 LOC)
Query planning. resolve.rs (name resolution, alias binding, column refs, star expansion, subquery scoping), where_clause.rs (index-usable terms, range constraints, OR optimization), join.rs (join ordering, beam search best-first mxChoice=12/18 matching wherePathSolver), cost.rs (I/O cost per access path, selectivity from sqlite_stat1/stat4), index.rs (usability, covering detection), plan.rs (QueryPlan output).

### fsqlite-vdbe (~6,000 LOC)
Bytecode VM, largest crate. vm.rs (fetch-execute loop, match dispatch, PC management), mem.rs (Mem/sqlite3_value, multi-representation, affinity, comparison), cursor.rs (VdbeCursor wrapping BtCursor, deferred seek, cached row decode, pseudo-table), program.rs (VdbeProgram Vec<VdbeOp>, register allocation, coroutine state), op.rs (VdbeOp struct, P4 enum), sort.rs (external merge sort), compare.rs (record comparison with collation), func_dispatch.rs (scalar/aggregate/window dispatch), subtype.rs (JSON subtype management).

### fsqlite-func (~2,500 LOC)
~80 built-in functions. scalar.rs (~60: abs, char, hex, instr, length, lower...), aggregate.rs (~12: avg, count, sum, group_concat...), window.rs (~11: row_number, rank, lag, lead...), math.rs (acos, sin, sqrt, log...), info.rs (sqlite_version, changes, total_changes, last_insert_rowid), registry.rs (FunctionRegistry: (name, arg_count) -> impl).

### Extensions
- fsqlite-ext-json (~2,000 LOC): JSON1, json(), json_extract/set/remove/type/valid, json_each/tree vtabs, JSONB, ->/-->>
- fsqlite-ext-fts5 (~4,000 LOC): FTS5, Porter stemmer, unicode61, inverted index, BM25, highlight/snippet, custom tokenizer API
- fsqlite-ext-fts3 (~2,000 LOC): FTS3/4 compat, matchinfo/offsets/snippet, wraps FTS5
- fsqlite-ext-rtree (~2,000 LOC): R*-tree spatial index, nearest-neighbor, geopoly
- fsqlite-ext-session (~1,500 LOC): Changeset/patchset gen/apply/invert
- fsqlite-ext-icu (~800 LOC): ICU collation, unicode comparison, case folding, FTS tokenizer
- fsqlite-ext-misc (~1,500 LOC): generate_series, dbstat, dbpage, csv vtab, decimal, uuid, ieee754, carray

### fsqlite-core (~5,000 LOC)
Orchestration layer. connection.rs (Connection, open/close, ATTACH/DETACH, schema cache, auto-commit, busy handler, auth callback), prepare.rs (SQL pipeline: parse->resolve->plan->codegen, LRU statement cache), schema.rs (sqlite_master loading, Table/Index/View/Trigger objects, schema cookie), codegen.rs (AST->VDBE for SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, expression codegen, subquery/CTE coroutines), pragma.rs (~80 pragmas), auth.rs (authorization dispatch), vtab.rs (virtual table registration/lifecycle).

### fsqlite (~1,000 LOC)
Public API facade. Database wraps Connection. Re-exports Statement, Row, Transaction, SqliteValue, PageNumber, FrankenError, ErrorCode, Result, Vfs, VfsFile, MemoryVfs. Convenience: open(), open_in_memory(), execute(cx, sql).await, query_row(cx, sql).await.

### fsqlite-cli (~2,000 LOC)
Interactive shell via frankentui. Dot-commands (.tables, .schema, .mode, .import, .dump, .headers, .separator). Output modes (column, csv, json, line, list, table). Tab completion, syntax highlighting, history.

### fsqlite-harness (~1,500 LOC)
Conformance test runner. Runs identical SQL against FrankenSQLite + C sqlite3. Row-by-row comparison. Error code matching. Golden file management.

 {"id":"bd-t6sv2.10","title":"Connection Pooling Best Practices & Validator","description":"**Idea #10 - Phase 3**

Guide users toward optimal connection management for concurrency. This bead is primarily guidance and validation tooling, building on transaction lifecycle data from bd-t6sv2.5.

**Components:**
1. **PoolPatternDetector**
   - Detect anti-patterns: single connection for concurrent use (serialized writes)
   - Identify over-pooling: more connections than useful (too many snapshots block GC)
   - Find connection leaks: long-idle connections holding snapshots
   - Detect rapid connect/disconnect cycling (connection thrashing)

2. **PoolAdvisor**
   - Recommend pool size based on workload: min(CPU_cores, write_concurrency_needed)
   - Suggest connection lifecycle settings (max idle time, max age)
   - Warn about statement preparation patterns (unprepared queries in hot loops)
   - Use transaction lifecycle data from bd-t6sv2.5 for recommendations

3. **BestPracticesDoc**
   - Connection pooling guide specific to FrankenSQLite MVCC model
   - Key insight: unlike C SQLite, FrankenSQLite benefits from multiple writer connections
   - Comparison with C SQLite patterns (single-writer pool vs MVCC multi-writer pool)
   - Integration examples for common Rust pool libraries (sqlx, r2d2, deadpool, bb8)

4. **PoolSimulator**
   - Test harness for pool configurations
   - Model concurrent connection behavior under various pool sizes
   - Find optimal pool size empirically for given workload
   - Uses benchmark infrastructure from bd-t6sv2.2

**Implementation:**
- Connection-level metrics in `fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` (connect/disconnect tracking, idle time)
- Analysis utilities in fsqlite-observability (pool pattern analysis)
- PRAGMA fsqlite_connection_stats: pool_size_estimate, idle_connections, connection_age_max_ms
- Documentation in docs/connection-pooling.md

**Files to Modify:**
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs (connection lifecycle tracking)
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/pragma.rs (connection stats PRAGMA)
- crates/fsqlite-observability/ (pool pattern detection)
- docs/connection-pooling.md (comprehensive guide)

**CRITICAL: MVCC Preservation**
Pool recommendations MUST emphasize that FrankenSQLite benefits from multiple writer connections (unlike C SQLite). Never suggest a single-writer pool as a "safe default" - that defeats the entire purpose of MVCC. Connection tracking MUST NOT introduce connection-level serialization.

**Test Requirements:**
- Unit: anti-pattern detection accuracy
  - Log: pattern_type (single_conn|over_pool|conn_leak|conn_thrash), confidence_score, evidence
  - Verify: single-connection-for-concurrent-use correctly detected
  - Verify: no false positives for healthy pool configurations
- Unit: pool size recommendation calculation
  - Log: workload_profile (read_heavy|write_heavy|mixed), recommended_pool_size, rationale
  - Verify: recommendation increases with write concurrency need
  - Verify: recommendation bounded by diminishing returns threshold
- Unit: connection lifecycle tracking
  - Log: conn_id, connect_ts, idle_since_ts, queries_executed, txn_count
  - Verify: lifecycle tracking accurate under concurrent connection use
- Integration: pool pattern detection under simulated workloads
  - Log: pool_size, concurrent_writers, conflicts_detected, recommendation
  - Scenario: single connection with 4 threads -> detect anti-pattern
  - Scenario: 100 connections with 4 threads -> detect over-pooling
  - Scenario: connections never closed -> detect leak pattern
- Integration: advisor recommendations improve throughput
  - Log: pool_size_before, throughput_before, pool_size_after, throughput_after, improvement_pct
  - Verify: following pool size recommendation improves throughput
- E2E: documentation examples execute correctly
  - Log: example_name, execution_result (pass|fail), error_if_any
  - Verify: each code example in docs compiles and produces expected output
  - Verify: pool configuration examples demonstrate correct behavior
- E2E: pool simulator produces consistent recommendations
  - Log: workload_params, simulated_throughput, recommended_config, confidence
  - Verify: simulator recommendations stable across runs (within noise)

**Verification Script:**
Create scripts/verify_pool_advisor.sh that:
1. Creates pools of various sizes with concurrent workload
2. Runs anti-pattern detection on each configuration
3. Verifies advisor recommendations match expected patterns
4. Tests documentation examples for correctness
5. Outputs pass/fail with recommendation accuracy stats

**Success Criteria:**

 {"id":"bd-x1ww","title":"§4.3.1 E-Process Framework: Ville's Inequality + Betting Martingales","description":"Implement the core e-process statistical monitoring engine for FrankenSQLite's MVCC invariants (§4.3, spec lines ~3997-4130).

SCOPE AND PURPOSE: E-processes based on Ville's inequality provide anytime-valid statistical monitoring — you can peek at any time during execution without inflating type-I error, unlike classical hypothesis testing. Under the null hypothesis H0 (invariant holds, violation probability <= p0), the e-process is a non-negative supermartingale starting at 1. Rejection occurs when E_t >= 1/alpha at any stopping time, with guaranteed type-I error control.

KEY DATA STRUCTURES AND APIs:
- EProcessConfig: {p0: f64, lambda: f64, alpha: f64, max_evalue: f64} — per-invariant calibration parameters.
- EProcess: {name, config, e_value (starts 1.0), observations count, rejected flag} — single fixed-lambda betting martingale.
- MixtureEProcess: {name, components: Vec<(weight, EProcess)>, alpha, log_components: Vec<f64>} — mixture of 16-64 lambda values on a log grid for near-oracle power without per-invariant hand-tuning.

ALGORITHMS:
- Fixed-lambda betting martingale update: E_t = E_{t-1} * (1 + lambda * (X_t - p0)), where X_t in {0,1}.
- Lambda constraint: lambda in (-1/(1-p0), 1/p0) for non-negativity.
- Mixture e-process: E_mix(t) = sum_j w_j * E_{lambda_j}(t), computed in log-space (log-sum-exp) for numerical stability.
- Expected detection delay: N_detect ~ log(1/alpha) / KL(p1 || p0).
- Alpha budget via union bound (sum alpha_i <= alpha_total) or e-value aggregation E_global(t) = sum_i w_i * E_i(t) — valid regardless of dependence between monitors (Vovk & Wang 2021).

CONFIGURATION PARAMETERS: p0 (null violation rate), lambda (bet size), alpha (significance level), max_evalue (overflow cap, e.g. 1e18). Per-invariant calibration is required — identical params for all invariants is explicitly wrong.

ERROR HANDLING: Lambda out of valid range must panic or error. E-value capped at max_evalue to prevent f64 overflow. Log-space computation must prevent NaN/Inf.

UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS (10 tests): (1) Initial state: e_value==1.0, observations==0, rejected==false. (2) No violations over 10K obs stays near 1.0. (3) Single violation jump ~2.0x for catastrophic config. (4) Systematic violations at p1=0.01 vs p0=0.001 rejects within 2K obs. (5) Mixture of 16 components: no false alarm under null, rejects within 500 obs under alternative. (6) Lambda constraint enforcement. (7) Log-space stability over 100K alternating obs — no NaN/Inf/negatives. (8) Alpha budget union bound: 7 monitors, sum(alpha_i)=0.01, 100K obs, no false rejections. (9) E-value aggregation with correlated observations rejects and identifies contributors. (10) Max e-value cap prevents overflow.

E2E TEST: Run e-process under H0 data (no rejection), then inject sustained violations and verify rejection with evidence entry.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: All martingale invariants hold (non-negative, starts at 1, supermartingale under H0). Mixture provides near-oracle power. Log-space computation is numerically stable over 100K+ observations. Union bound and aggregation correctly control family-wise error. Proof certificates include monitor name, final e-value, threshold, observation count.","notes":"All 10 required tests implemented in crates/fsqlite-harness/src/eprocess.rs (22 total tests): initial_state, no_violations_stays_near_one, single_violation_jumps, repeated_violations_reject, mixture_no_lambda_tuning, lambda_constraint_enforced, log_space_stability, alpha_budget_union_bound, evalue_aggregation_rejects_correlated, max_evalue_cap. **Spec Reference:** COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENSQLITE_V1.md §4.3, lines 3997–4130

## Overview

Implement the core e-process engine that provides anytime-valid statistical monitoring
for FrankenSQLite's MVCC invariants. E-processes (based on Ville's inequality) allow
peeking at any time during execution without inflating type-I error — unlike classical
hypothesis testing, no correction for multiple testing over time is needed.

## Formal Definition

An **e-process** `(E_t)_{t >= 0}` is a sequence of random variables adapted to a
filtration `(F_t)` such that:

1. `E_0 = 1` (starts at one)
2. `E_t >= 0` for all `t` (non-negative)
3. `E[E_t | F_{t-1}] <= E_{t-1}` (supermartingale under the null hypothesis H_0)

The null hypothesis H_0 asserts that the invariant holds (violation probability is
at most `p_0`, typically 0.001).

**Ville's inequality (key property):** For any stopping time `tau` and significance
level `alpha`:

```
P_{H_0}(exists t : E_t >= 1/alpha) <= alpha
```

This means you can peek at any time and reject H_0 (conclude the invariant is
systematically violated) if `E_t >= 1/alpha`, without inflating the type-I error rate.

## Fixed-λ Betting Martingale

The betting martingale update rule:

```
E_t = E_{t-1} * (1 + lambda * (X_t - p_0))
```

where:
- `lambda` is the bet size, constrained to `(-1/(1-p_0), 1/p_0)` for non-negativity
- `X_t` is the observation (1 = violation, 0 = no violation)
- `p_0` is the null hypothesis violation rate

Under H_0, `E[X_t] = p_0`, so `E[E_t | E_{t-1}] = E_{t-1}` (martingale).
Under the alternative H_1 (actual violation rate `p_1 > p_0`), the e-process grows
exponentially at rate `KL(p_1 || p_0)` per observation.

## Mixture E-Processes (Recommended Alien-Artifact Upgrade)

Fixed-λ is valid but brittle. Any nonnegative mixture of valid e-processes is itself
a valid e-process (by linearity of expectation):

```
E_mix(t) := Σ_j w_j * E_{λ_j}(t),   w_j >= 0, Σ_j w_j = 1
```

where each `E_{λ_j}` updates as `E_t = E_{t-1} * (1 + λ_j (X_t - p0))`.

### Practical Implementation (Normative):
- Choose `λ_j` on a **log grid** spanning "sensitive to rare violations" through
  "sensitive to frequent violations" (e.g., **16–64 values**)
- Maintain `log(E_{λ_j})` and compute the mixture in **log-space** (log-sum-exp)
  for numerical stability
- Alarm when `E_mix(t) >= 1/alpha` (same Ville guarantee; optional stopping safe)

This gives near-oracle power across a wide range of `p_1` without per-invariant
hand-tuning, while preserving the same statistical guarantee under H0.

## Alpha Budget (Union Bound) for Multiple Monitors

FrankenSQLite runs many monitors (INV-1..INV-7, INV-SSI-FP, symbol survival,
replication divergence, etc.). Two approaches for family-wise error control:

1. **Alpha budget (union bound, simplest):** choose per-monitor levels `alpha_i`
   such that `sum_i alpha_i <= alpha_total`. Each monitor rejects when
   `E_i(t) >= 1/alpha_i`.

2. **E-value aggregation (adaptive, recommended):** choose weights `w_i >= 0`
   with `sum_i w_i = 1` and define:

   ```
   E_global(t) := Σ_i w_i * E_i(t)
   ```

   This is a valid e-process under the global null **regardless of dependence**
   between monitors (Vovk & Wang 2021, §4). Critical because MVCC invariant
   monitors observe the same transactions and are therefore correlated.

   The resulting certificate includes top contributing monitors by `w_i * E_i(t)`
   share (an "evidence ledger").

   *Note:* Weighted geometric mean `Π_i E_i(t)^{w_i}` would be tighter but
   requires conditional independence (which does not hold here). Arithmetic mean
   is the standard dependence-robust aggregation.

## Implementation Structs

```rust
use asupersync::lab::oracle::eprocess::{EProcess, EProcessConfig};

pub struct EProcessConfig {
    pub p0: f64,        // Null hypothesis violation rate
    pub lambda: f64,    // Bet size (fixed-λ) or unused if mixture
    pub alpha: f64,     // Significance level
    pub max_evalue: f64, // Cap to prevent f64 overflow
}

pub struct EProcess {
    pub name: &'static str,
    pub config: EProcessConfig,
    pub e_value: f64,          // Current e-value (starts at 1.0)
    pub observations: u64,     // Count of observations
    pub rejected: bool,        // Whether H0 has been rejected
}

pub struct MixtureEProcess {
    pub name: &'static str,
    pub components: Vec<(f64, EProcess)>,  // (weight, component)
    pub alpha: f64,
    pub log_components: Vec<f64>,          // log-space for stability
}
```

## Expected Detection Delay Formula

For a monitor with `p0` and `lambda`, the expected detection delay when true
violation rate is `p1`:

```
N_detect ≈ log(1/alpha) / KL(p1 || p0)
```

where `KL(p1 || p0) = p1 * ln(p1/p0) + (1-p1) * ln((1-p1)/(1-p0))`.

## Unit Test Specifications

### Test 1: `test_eprocess_initial_state`
Verify that a newly created EProcess has `e_value == 1.0`, `observations == 0`,
and `rejected == false`.

### Test 2: `test_eprocess_no_violations_stays_near_one`
Feed 10,000 `X_t = 0` (no violation) observations to an EProcess with
`p0 = 0.001, lambda = 0.9`. Assert `e_value` stays within `[0.0, 2.0]`
(it should decay slightly under pure null, since each step multiplies by
`(1 - lambda * p0)`).

### Test 3: `test_eprocess_single_violation_jump`
With `lambda = 0.999, p0 = 1e-9`, observe one violation (`X_t = 1`).
The e-value should jump by approximately `(1 + lambda * (1 - p0)) ≈ 2.0`.
Assert `e_value` is in `[1.9, 2.1]`.

### Test 4: `test_eprocess_rejects_on_systematic_violations`
Feed violations at rate `p1 = 0.01` (10x the null rate of `p0 = 0.001`) with
`lambda = 0.5, alpha = 0.05`. After enough observations, `rejected` must be
`true`. Expected detection delay: `log(20) / KL(0.01, 0.001) ≈ 430` observations.
Feed 2000 and assert rejection occurred.

### Test 5: `test_mixture_eprocess_valid`
Create a mixture of 16 components on a log grid of λ values from 0.01 to 0.99.
Feed 5000 observations with no violations. Assert `E_mix` stays below `1/alpha`.
Then feed violations at rate 0.05 and assert rejection occurs within 500 observations.

### Test 6: `test_lambda_constraint_enforced`
Attempt to create an EProcess with `lambda` outside `(-1/(1-p0), 1/p0)`.
Assert that creation panics or returns an error.

### Test 7: `test_log_space_stability`
Create a mixture e-process and feed 100,000 observations alternating between
violation and no-violation. Assert no NaN, no infinity, and no negative e-values
at any point during the sequence.

### Test 8: `test_alpha_budget_union_bound`
Create 7 monitors with `alpha_i` such that `sum(alpha_i) = 0.01`. Under the
global null (no real violations in any monitor), run 100,000 observations each.
Assert that no monitor rejects (with overwhelming probability).

### Test 9: `test_evalue_aggregation_rejects_correlated`
Create 4 monitors with equal weights `w_i = 0.25`. Feed correlated observations
(all monitors see the same violations). Assert that `E_global` rejects and the
evidence ledger correctly identifies the contributing monitors.

### Test 10: `test_max_evalue_cap`
With `max_evalue = 1e18`, feed enough violations to drive `e_value` past the cap.
Assert it is clamped to `max_evalue` and does not overflow to infinity.

## Purpose

This sub-epic ensures that Phase 5 wiring doesn't break existing functionality and that
the new storage stack works correctly end-to-end. It provides:

1. **Regression guard** — existing 5,319 tests must still pass after each phase
2. **End-to-end validation** — comprehensive test script exercising the full pipeline
3. **C SQLite compatibility** — .db files must be readable by both implementations
4. **Crash recovery** — WAL replay must produce consistent state after simulated crash

## Why this is critical

Phase 5 changes the fundamental data flow of the system. Without rigorous testing:
- Subtle data corruption could go unnoticed until production use
- Performance regressions could mask correctness issues
- WAL/pager interactions may have edge cases only visible under load
- C SQLite compatibility is essential for migration and interop

## Logging philosophy

All tests in Phase 5F use **structured logging** (not just println/eprintln):
- Log format: `[PHASE][TEST_NAME][STEP] message | key1=value1 key2=value2`
- Timing: every significant operation is timed with `Instant::now()` / `elapsed()`
- Assertions log expected vs actual before asserting
- On failure: dump full database state (page count, WAL size, btree structure)
- Test harness captures logs to file for post-mortem analysis

## Children

- bd-16e7: 5F.1 — Per-phase regression guard
- bd-jd39: 5F.2 — End-to-end storage stack test script
- bd-3r48: 5F.3 — C SQLite file format compatibility
- bd-3tc7: 5F.4 — Crash recovery and WAL replay test

-{"id":"bd-xb07w","title":"PERF-W5-2: Wire IMPL-17 morsel-driven INSERT primitive into production dispatch path","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:35:57.959929465Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:37:25.301317770Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-xb07w","depends_on_id":"bd-t2sf9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:36:08.816281918Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3556,"issue_id":"bd-xb07w","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Goal
The IMPL-17 morsel-driven parallel INSERT primitive landed at `5a9de592` as a scaffolding primitive. It is NOT currently consumed by the production INSERT path. Wire it in.

## Current state
- Primitive exists in `fsqlite-mvcc`: `feat(mvcc): morsel-driven parallel INSERT scheduling primitive (AG-3B / IMPL-24 follow-up)`.
- Direct-simple INSERT (`737d7a45` T1.3/IMPL-1) and batched INSERT paths do NOT call into the morsel scheduler.

## Target paths
- `execute_prepared_direct_simple_insert` and friends in `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs`
- Multi-row `INSERT ... VALUES (...), (...), ...` dispatch
- `INSERT ... SELECT` with large row counts

## Heuristic for when to morsel
- row count >= MORSEL_THRESHOLD (start with 64 rows)
- Fixed-width columns preferred (morsel concurrency has overhead; small fast rows benefit most)

## Measurement
- New bench: `bulk_insert_morsel_bench` at 100/1000/10000 rows across 1/4/8 morsel threads.
- Compare vs current single-thread INSERT, vs SQLite.
- Target: >=2x speedup at 1000 rows with 4 morsels vs current single-thread.

## Correctness guards
- Morsel writes must respect concurrent-mode MVCC snapshot — each morsel gets its own txn slice, merge at commit.
- Autoincrement rowid allocation: reserve a range up-front and distribute to morsels.
- RETURNING clause: must preserve row order.
- Triggers: BEFORE/AFTER INSERT triggers interact with rowid ordering — may need to disable morsel path when triggers present.
